studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention group uses the ViViRA Neck app for 12 weeks; the control group receives at least 6 sessions of individual physiotherapy over 12 weeks. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Smartphone app ViViRA Neck with access for 12 weeks; Control group: Standard of care, i.e., at least 1 prescription with 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks. Planned total: 214 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Use of the CE-marked app 'ViViRA Neck' for 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care physiotherapy — at least 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain intensity measured by Visual Analogue Scale (VAS, 0-100) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Disability measured by Neck Disability Index (NDI) at 12 weeks; Health-related quality of life measured by Veterans Rand 12-item Health Survey (VR12) at 12 weeks.

studies.duration_additional_info: The study ran for 12 weeks. Patients in the intervention group exercised with the ViViRA app at least 3 times per week for 20 minutes. Measurements were collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: patients with axial spondyloarthritis or axial psoriatic arthritis who received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and exercised for 12 weeks (at least 3x/week, 20 min/session), plus an activity sensor wristband. Control group: patients with the same diagnosis who underwent physiotherapy at least once a week as standard of care. Total enrolled: 70 (planned: 75). Exact per-group sizes not specified.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and were instructed to exercise for 12 weeks, optimally 3 times per week for 20 minutes, with back-specific exercises. They also received a wristband activity sensor. Arm 2 (Control): Patients underwent physiotherapy as standard of care at least once a week for 12 weeks. Both groups had clinical measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: pain reduction, change in range of motion (BASMI score), change in gait pattern, change in clinical parameters. Secondary outcomes: differences in gait pattern and disease activity between groups; influence of ViViRA on movement competence, sleep quality, hand strength, work productivity, pain, fear of movement, and quality of life. Instruments/measures collected: BASMI (spinal mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, ASAS 20 (disease activity), BSA, WPAI (work productivity), PSQI (sleep quality), ASqL, SF-36 (quality of life), HAQ, PAIN scale, TSK (fear of movement), IPAQ, PAHCO (movement competence), gait and movement analysis with shoe-attached sensors, physical activity via wristband activity sensor, hand strength measurement, clinical routine blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP), physical examination.

studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome (pain reduction via VNRS) was assessed at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. The trial ran from November 2020 to March 2021.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Vivira app-based personalized home exercise therapy) vs Arm 2 (Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists per German statutory health insurance agreements); total N=213
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app — digitally adapted guideline-based exercises and patient education. Arm 2: Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists according to agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Verbal Numeric Rating Scale) at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Function improvement of mobility.

studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and after 12 weeks as the primary outcomes.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA; control group received standard physiotherapy. Total analyzed: 59 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): SpAs patients received the ViViRA digital health application for home-based physiotherapy. Arm 2 (Control): SpAs patients received standard physiotherapy.
studies.collected_data: Pain assessed via verbal rating scale (VRS) and PAIN-Detect questionnaire; quality of life assessed via SF-36; spinal mobility assessed via BASMI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index). All measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Data from 59 participants (71.2% female, mean age 45.2 years) were analyzed. The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline 1.1 [range 0.7–1.5]; follow-up 1.0 [range 0.5–1.4]; p=0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline 1.5 [range 1.1–1.9]; follow-up 1.8 [range 1.4–2.2]; p=0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks. The authors concluded that DHA use resulted in superior outcomes compared with standard physiotherapy for lower back pain in SpAs patients.

studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months (primary endpoint at 6 months, with interim at 3 months)
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants exercised at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: ViViRA with personal coaching (intervention 1), Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (intervention 2), ViViRA without coaching (control). Total N=78; individual group sizes not specified.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: ViViRA DHA with personalized (human) coaching – one-time 30-min online coaching session prior to DHA use. Arm 2: Kaia Health DHA with AI-based coaching – video-based AI integrated into DHA providing movement guidance during each session. Arm 3 (Control): ViViRA DHA without any coaching. All arms instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months.
studies.collected_data: Sociodemographic data; Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI, range 0-10, lower = better mobility); PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range 0-38, higher = more severe pain); PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher = better competence); adherence (weekly use of DHA). Collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed significant improvement in mobility (BASMI): baseline to 3 months mean change -0.6 to -0.7, all p < 0.001. Pain intensity (PainDETECT) decreased substantially in all arms: baseline to 6 months mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points, all p ≤ 0.006. PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (baseline to 6 months: +1.02, p = 0.013), but did not exceed other interventions in direct comparison. No coaching strategy showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months (2-3 weekly DHA use).

studies.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome was assessed at 12 weeks, corresponding to the duration of the intervention program. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control arm: standard of care physiotherapy consisting of 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at baseline and 12 weeks. Clinical relevance thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%. Retention rates were also tracked.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Mean difference in pain scores between groups at 12 weeks: -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. Intervention group achieved a clinically relevant pain reduction: mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5). Control group did not reach clinical relevance: mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates: 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity superior to standard physiotherapy.

studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividually controlled study. The study is single-arm with intra-individual control (baseline/historical findings of the same patient serve as control). Data collection period: June 1–15, 2020.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm of 179 patients using the Vivira app; intra-individual control (each patient's own baseline/historical findings serve as the control).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study: all participants received the Vivira digital home exercise app. The control is the intra-individual historical (baseline) findings of the same patient.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain assessed using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test scores. Descriptive variables including measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividual controlled study. The primary outcome (VNRS pain score) is assessed after 12 weeks. The study analyzed data from patients using the Vivira app from 01/01/2020 onwards. Data analysis was conducted between 2021-06-15 and 2021-06-22.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study (N=157 analyzed, 150 planned). Intraindividual control: the control is the pre-treatment (historical/baseline) finding of the same patient. Intervention: use of the Vivira digital home exercise app.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app. Single-armed study with intraindividual control (baseline/pre-finding of the same patient serves as control).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: VNRS (verbal-numerical rating scale) of pain after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test (series of functional scores). Descriptive variables including measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

studies.duration_additional_info: The digital therapeutic consists of a twelve-week individualized home exercise program. The study is a single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison. Actual data collection ran from 2022-04-06 to 2022-05-10.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; no control group. 7842 patients with non-specific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain using the digital therapeutic 'Vivira' (adaptive home exercise program with individualized exercise progressions).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (DiGA 'Vivira').
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Self-reported pain intensity assessed with a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS, 0-10). Secondary outcomes: (1) Clinical relevance of pain intensity improvement assessed via responder analysis using three criteria established in the literature (criterion met / criterion not met); (2) Physical functional capacity assessed in the dimensions of mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale; (3) Patient characteristics associated with good treatment adherence.

studies.duration_additional_info: Pain intensity was assessed at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. The observation period was 12 weeks total.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm observational study; 3629 patients using the Vivira digital home exercise program (no separate control group).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' (a fully approved DiGA) for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. No comparator arm.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured with a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: self-reported function scores for back, hip, and knee pain areas, assessed using matched-pair analysis (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). Attrition/follow-up rates were also tracked.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test: T3628=5308; P<.001), with changes within the range of clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive but more variable response across back, hip, and knee pain areas. Relevant attrition at follow-up was noted as a challenge.

studies.duration_additional_info: The study assessed pain score changes over the intended 12-week duration of the program. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Observational study using an incomplete matched block design; no separate control group described. All 2517 participants used the Vivira smartphone-based home exercise program.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm observational study. All participants used Vivira, a smartphone-based program for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. A matched comparison of self-reported functional scores and adherence rates was also presented.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (0-10 scale), self-reported functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), adherence rates. Measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Overall, initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19/10 (SD 1.96) to 3.35/10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores; participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and pain durations.

studies.duration_additional_info: The study does not specify the duration of the program or when the primary endpoint was assessed.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Groups defined by use profiles: High Use (HU), Intermediate Use (IU), Low Use (LU), and Sub-Low Use (sub-LU). Specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Four use profile groups were compared: High Use (HU), Intermediate Use (IU), Low Use (LU), and Sub-Low Use (sub-LU) of a digital home exercise program for nonspecific back pain (NSBP). Propensity Score Matching (PSM) was used to comparatively assess outcomes across these groups.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity; clinically relevant pain improvement (using commonly accepted thresholds for clinically relevant improvements in sensitivity analyses); use/adherence profiles of a digital home exercise program.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles were associated with a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement in self-reported pain intensities. Achieving any higher use level was associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement. The study concluded that a High Use (HU) profile should be pursued to enable optimal effectiveness of DTx home exercise programs.

audit.study_title: Prospective randomized controlled trial to evaluate the clinical effectiveness of the app-based therapeutic training program (ViViRA Neck) for patients with non-specific neck pain.
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Randomized controlled trial to assess the effectiveness of the ViViRA digital health app on pain reduction and range of motion in inflammatory low back pain
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title:
Prospective Randomized Group Controlled Study 
of Clinical Efficacy and Safety of the 
CE marked Vivira App “In-Home Therapeutic Training Program”
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Impact of the digital health application ViViRA on spinal mobility, physical function, quality of life and pain perception in spondyloarthritides patients: a randomized controlled trial.
Impact of personalized coaching on the use of digital health interventions for movement therapy in rheumatology: a randomized controlled trial.
The Effect of an App-Based Home Exercise Program on Self-reported Pain Intensity in Unspecific and Degenerative Back Pain: Pragmatic Open-label Randomized Controlled Trial.

Preparation of a clinical report on anonymized, statistically evaluated Data from a retrospective data collection on the clinical efficacy of the CE-marked Vivira app "Therapeutic exercise program for home use"
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Preparation of a clinical report 2020-2021 on anonymized, statistically analyzed data of a retrospective data collection on the clinical efficacy of the CE-marked Vivira app "Therapeutic Exercise Program for Home Use"
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Post-marketing effectiveness of a digital therapeutic for unspecific and degenerative back, hip and knee pain during an extended probation period: An observational study of patient-reported outcome data
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Postmarketing Follow-Up of a Digital Home Exercise Program for Back, Hip, and Knee Pain: Retrospective Observational Study With a Time-Series and Matched-Pair Analysis.
Preliminary Use and Outcome Data of a Digital Home Exercise Program for Back, Hip, and Knee Pain: Retrospective Observational Study With a Time Series and Matched Analysis.
The Effect of Therapeutic Adherence on the Effectiveness of a Digital Therapeutic Exercise Program: A Propensity Score Matching Analysis.
